subject: ENGLISH
title: Cause-and-Effect Garden
objective: Distinguish cause from effect and connect them clearly.
introduction: A cause helps make something happen; an effect is what happens. Sequence alone does not prove causation. You will explore examples, sort cards, practice, and answer five questions.

vocabulary.definition: A key word used in the lesson “Cause-and-Effect Garden”.
A key word used in the lesson “Cause-and-Effect Garden”.

activities.instruction: Study the idea and listen to the narration.
activities.content: A cause helps make something happen; an effect is what happens. Sequence alone does not prove causation.
activities.accessibilityAlternative: Text alternative: A cause helps make something happen; an effect is what happens. Sequence alone does not prove causation.

activities.instruction: Explore each learning token, then read the examples.

examples: It rained, so the path became wet.
The plant wilted because it lacked water.
The bell rang, so learners lined up.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: A numbered list of the same examples is available for keyboard and screen-reader use.

activities.instruction: Sort each card into “fits the lesson” or “does not fit.”

fits: It rained, so the path became wet.
The plant wilted because it lacked water.
The bell rang, so learners lined up.
doesNotFit: Because it rained, so the path got wet.

feedback.correct: Your groups follow the lesson rule.
feedback.retry: Use the explanation, not color or position.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: Tap an item and then tap a labeled category; dragging is optional.

activities.instruction: What is the effect in “The wind blew, so the leaves moved”?

feedback.retry: Read the lesson rule again. The wind blew. and The word so. do not fit.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: All choices are visible text and selectable with TalkBack.

activities.instruction: Match each example to the lesson idea.

pairs.left: It rained, so the path became wet.
The plant wilted because it lacked water.
The bell rang, so learners lined up.

feedback.retry: Return to the explanation and compare one pair at a time.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: A list-based matching control replaces drag gestures.

activities.instruction: Complete the guided review in order.

feedback.correct: You are ready for the five-question check.

activities.accessibilityAlternative: Up and down buttons provide an alternative to dragging.

options.text: A cause helps make something happen; an effect is what happens. Sequence alone does not prove causation.
Because it rained, so the path got wet.

items.explanation: The correct response is “A cause helps make something happen; an effect is what happens. Sequence alone does not prove causation.” because it follows the lesson explanation: A cause helps make something happen; an effect is what happens. Sequence alone does not prove causation.

options.text: It rained, so the path became wet.
Because it rained, so the path got wet.

items.explanation: The correct response is “It rained, so the path became wet.” because it follows the lesson explanation: A cause helps make something happen; an effect is what happens. Sequence alone does not prove causation.

items.prompt: Which choice does not follow the lesson idea?

options.text: Because it rained, so the path got wet.
It rained, so the path became wet.
The plant wilted because it lacked water.

items.explanation: The correct response is “Because it rained, so the path got wet.” because it follows the lesson explanation: A cause helps make something happen; an effect is what happens. Sequence alone does not prove causation.

items.prompt: What is the effect in “The wind blew, so the leaves moved”?
items.explanation: The correct response is “The leaves moved.” because it follows the lesson explanation: A cause helps make something happen; an effect is what happens. Sequence alone does not prove causation.

items.prompt: Which example should be used in the final review?

options.text: The bell rang, so learners lined up.
Because it rained, so the path got wet.

items.explanation: The correct response is “The bell rang, so learners lined up.” because it follows the lesson explanation: A cause helps make something happen; an effect is what happens. Sequence alone does not prove causation.

assetSpecs.prompt: Friendly Grade 3 cartoon storybook concept board for Cause-and-Effect Garden; rounded shapes, warm dark-brown outlines, tropical palette, no embedded text, uncluttered small-screen layout. Compose overlays provide labels and interactive targets.
assetSpecs.contentDescription: Five connected learning tokens for exploring examples in Cause-and-Effect Garden.
